title: Weaning kits and returning the doe to breeding
subtitle: The eight-week mark, how to separate without stress, and the timing of the next mating.
excerpt: Weaning is the point where a litter becomes independent and the doe can be bred again. Done badly it stresses both doe and kits and can trigger mastitis or digestive upset in the litter. A guide to the correct age for weaning, how to separate the litter, and when to return the doe to the buck.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits, Crown copyright. University of Wisconsin Extension, rabbit production series. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), rabbit chapter, Project Gutenberg.

glossaryTerms.definition: A newborn or young rabbit, from birth to weaning at around 8 weeks.
A female rabbit kept for breeding. She carries and feeds each litter and controls her own territory; buck and doe must be housed separately outside mating visits.
A male rabbit used for breeding. He is brought to the doe's cage for mating and removed immediately; placing a buck in the doe's own space causes fighting.

Inflammation of the mammary glands, often caused by bacterial infection. In rabbits it typically occurs when kits are weaned abruptly before milk production has slowed, or when a kit is lost and the doe cannot drain the gland.
To give birth in rabbits. A doe kindles in a nest she has lined with pulled fur.

content.text: can be separated and returned to the breeding cycle. The kits have been eating solid food for three to four weeks by this point. The doe's milk production is already winding down naturally, which reduces the risk of

Eight weeks is the recommended minimum for meat breeds. Do not wean at five or six weeks even if the kits look large enough. Early weaning stresses the digestive system before gut flora are established and raises the risk of enteritis, which kills fast.
Check the doe's mammary glands before separating. They should be soft with no hard or warm patches. A warm, hard patch suggests a blocked gland that may develop into
. If you find one, continue nursing for another two to three days and monitor. Contact a vet if the gland does not soften.

Move the doe to a separate cage, not the kits. The kits stay in familiar territory with familiar smells. Moving the doe reduces her stress far more than moving the whole litter to an unfamiliar space.

Keep the doe's new cage out of sight and smell of the litter for the first few days. Does will call and pace if they can hear or smell the kits. The litter will call less intensely than the doe and settle faster.
become sexually active from around 10 to 12 weeks. If you are not slaughtering the litter at 10 to 12 weeks, separate males from females before 10 weeks to prevent unwanted matings within the litter.

A doe can be remated 10 to 14 days after weaning, once she has had time to recover condition. Check her weight by feel along the backbone. If the spine is prominent, she is thin; give her a further two to three weeks on good hay and pellets before remating.
's cage for mating. Watch until you see two matings, then remove the doe. Do not leave them together unsupervised; does that are not receptive will fight. Note the mating date in your records.

attrs.title: Kindle date calculation
attrs.body: Rabbit gestation is 28 to 32 days, usually 31 days. Count forward from the mating date and mark a kindle box in place by day 28. The doe will start pulling fur and building a nest in the last two to three days of pregnancy.

content.text: A basic records card per doe tracks:

date, litter size, kits alive at 24 hours, weaning date, weight of litter at weaning, and remating date. Over several litters this shows you which does are the most productive and which are worth culling from the breeding programme.

items.symptom: Kits showing soft or watery droppings in the week after weaning
items.cause: Stress of separation combined with gut flora still developing
items.fix: Increase hay ration and reduce pellets for three to four days. Ensure fresh water is always available. If droppings are bloody or the kit becomes lethargic, contact a vet as enteritis progresses quickly.

items.symptom: Doe refusing to accept the buck after weaning
items.cause: Not yet fully recovered, or not at the right stage of her receptive cycle
items.fix: Check her body condition. If she is thin, give her more time. A doe showing a red or deep pink vulva is more likely to be receptive than one with a pale pink vulva. Try again in five to seven days.

items.symptom: Doe loses the litter shortly after remating (reabsorbs or aborts)
items.cause: Too soon after weaning, poor body condition, or stress
items.fix: Wait a full three weeks after the next weaning before remating. Check housing for signs of disturbance that may be stressing the doe at night.
